title: How We Lost Control of Our Food w/ Kyle Diamantas

chunks.title: Introduction to Food Dyes and Nutrition
chunks.summary: The discussion opens with a focus on food dyes, highlighting their main purpose to make foods appealing rather than providing any real nutritional benefit. It underlines that these additives are contributing to various health issues, suggesting that the consumer gains little from their presence in food.

chunks.title: FDA's Role and Perspective
chunks.summary: Kyle, the deputy commissioner of the FDA, shares his comprehensive background in the food industry, discussing the broken state of the FDA and food systems. He emphasizes the need for reform and how historical practices, such as the 'grass' loophole, have led to concerns over food safety.

chunks.title: Concerns Over Food Ingredients
chunks.summary: The conversation delves deeper into how the proliferation of additives and preservatives, especially from the late 20th century onward, has significantly changed food safety. The deputy commissioner explains the implications of these changes on health and how there was a lack of ongoing review processes for food additives.
chunks.transcript: years, right? But our food supply changed, right? In the 70s, 80s, 90s, even before that, there was a huge push for food security, to to add preservatives, to extend shelf life, to create products that were shelf stable. And again, like that wasn't done with ill intent. You know, we were doing it from a food safety perspective, a food security perspective, right? All these new ingredients, these preservatives, emulsifiers, additives were put into the food supply and they were largely done through the grass process, right? And Congress and FDA tried really hard to say, well, maybe we we have a a mandatory grass system where we have to do it. We have to review everything. It became unwieldy. FDA basically said, we can't do it. They allowed companies to essentially self-grass, which means they can hire their own experts. Theoretically, they're supposed to meet the exact same safety standards as a food additive petition, right? But companies could do it on their own. They didn't have to tell FDA. They didn't have to tell anybody. They didn't have to put their data out in the public domain. So, companies were able to essentially self-certify that an ingredient was grass, put it out to market, and FDA had no way of tracking that. We had no way of knowing. And so unfortunately the grass loophole as people call it sort of swallowed the rule itself and became the primary mechanism where ingredients were getting on the market. But we didn't necessarily know the long-term health effects of that. And we never really studied it the way we should have, right? The way FDA has historically worked in this space, once an ingredient was on the market, there wasn't a robust framework to continue to review that data. So if you look at drugs, you've got obligations in places to continually review the safety of data. Food never really had that formal process in place. chunks.title: New Framework for Food Safety
chunks.summary: Detailing new initiatives at the FDA, Kyle describes plans to establish a formal review process for existing additives, aiming to transition from a reactive to a proactive stance on food safety. He emphasizes the importance of monitoring chemicals of concern and addressing the 'grass' loophole.
chunks.transcript: to be proactive for new things coming in both. Okay. So, new things, new things coming in. We're going to address grass. We're not going to have a grass loophole. We're taking steps now to close that grass loophole on the back end, things already on the market. We're standing up a postmarket framework so that we could prioritize chemicals of concern in the food supply. So, we're going to put together a framework that's going to that's going to look at is there new safety data? Is there concerning data? What are other countries doing? What are consumers worried about? What are the states seeing? Right? or the states passing laws banning certain ingredients. So, we're going to create a framework. It helps us assess how we should prioritize our postmarket review and then we're going to start reviewing these things. We're going to expedite review and we're going to stand up for the first time a formal framework to review in the postmarket context ingredients that are already in the food supply while we also

chunks.title: Impact of Ultra-Processed Foods
chunks.summary: The segment discusses the alarming rise of ultraprocessed foods, which now comprise 60-70% of diets, and the associated health effects such as obesity and chronic diseases. There is a focus on how engineered food ingredients may contribute to these issues beyond just calorie intake.

chunks.title: Chronic Diseases and Food Ingredients
chunks.summary: The dialogue addresses the relationship between processed food ingredients and the rising rates of chronic diseases, advocating for a nuanced approach to understanding food environments and consumer behavior in relation to dietary health.
chunks.transcript: standards are probably harmful for us. Yeah. Look, the the reality is there's not one ingredient, not one chemical that's to blame. You can't blame just the dyes. You can't blame just preservatives. There is a chronic disease problem in our country that's associated with a lot of things. Our food ingredients part of that is the ultrarocessing, the stripping of wheat, the adding of these ingredients. Is that part of the problem? Absolutely. chunks.title: Consumer Demand and Industry Response
chunks.summary: Kyle notes how consumer demand for transparency and healthier ingredients is influencing industry practices. They discuss the role of agencies and social media in pushing for change and the subsequent need for federal regulations on food additives.
